title: Spin Glass Mapping of the Parallel Minority Game
authors: Aryan Tyagi, Soumyaditya Das, Soumyajyoti Biswas, Anirban Chakraborti
abstract: The parallel minority game (PMG) extends the classical minority game to many choices, with each agent restricted to two predetermined alternatives. In this condition, minimizing the population variance across all choices is a complex combinatorial optimization problem. We show that this minimization is exactly equivalent to finding the ground state of an Ising spin glass in the mean-field limit, i.e., the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model. By encoding the agent choices as spin variables, the variance becomes a quadratic Hamiltonian with quenched random couplings $J_{ij}$ and random fields $h_i$. This mapping reveals inherent frustration and connects the PMG to the well developed theory of spin glasses, providing a new perspective on the frozen, sub-optimal configurations observed in stochastic strategies.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the optimization problem in the Parallel Minority Game is mathematically equivalent to finding the ground state of a Sherrington-Kirkpatrick spin glass. This mapping provides a theoretical framework to understand the emergence of glassy, sub-optimal states in resource allocation problems. It also reveals that the system's energy function can saturate much faster than the actual population variance.
main_problem: The paper addresses the difficulty of minimizing population variance in the Parallel Minority Game (PMG) and seeks to understand the origin of 'frozen' sub-optimal configurations and glassy behavior in stochastic strategies.
main_result: The authors prove that minimizing population variance in the PMG is exactly equivalent to finding the ground state of an Ising spin glass (Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model) in the mean-field limit.
method: The study uses a mathematical mapping that encodes agent choices as binary spin variables to transform the population variance formula into a quadratic Ising Hamiltonian.
model_or_system: The system is the Parallel Minority Game (PMG), where N agents choose between two fixed locations from D possible sites. The problem is mapped to an Ising spin glass Hamiltonian with quenched random couplings (J_ij) and random fields (h_i).
key_observables: Population variance (efficiency metric), energy per spin (Hamiltonian value), and energy saturation time scales.
important_parameters: Number of agents (N), number of locations (D), target population (g), and switching probability (p_i).
main_assumptions: The mapping assumes a mean-field limit; the analysis of strategies assumes specific rules for how agents update their choices based on population information.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the time evolution of population variance for different strategies and target populations; Figure 2 shows the evolution of the spin glass Hamiltonian energy reaching saturation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the PMG problem, establishes the mathematical mapping to the Ising spin glass, analyzes the dynamics of different stochastic strategies, and discusses the implications of the energy-variance time scale discrepancy.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Variance evolution for D=101 and different values of g across time, for various strategies: A , B and C. The variance decreases initially and then stabilizes.

FIG. 2. Time evolution of the Hamiltonian (Eg. 7) for D=101 and different values of g, various strategies: A , B and C. The Hamiltonian decreases and reaches a saturation indicat- ing convergence to local minima of the spin energy landscape.
